result:
The sky tonight is not the velvet black of my childhood. It’s a washed-out indigo, diluted by the constant glow of the town below. I miss the darkness. True darkness is not an absence, but a presence—a thick, velvety blanket that settles over the hills, so complete it makes your other senses hum. In that dark, you could hear the rustle of a fox three fields over, smell the cool scent of dew on long grass, feel the immensity of space above you.

We’ve traded that profound darkness for convenience, for safety, for the small comfort of a streetlamp’s orange pool. And I understand why. But I wonder what we lose when we banish the night. Without the deep dark, we never truly look up. We lose the shock, the awe, the humbling scale of the Milky Way sprayed across the heavens. We forget our smallness in a good way. Our problems shrink under the gaze of a billion ancient stars.

Sometimes I drive out past the last service station, where the road narrows and the artificial light finally fades. I turn off the engine and step out. It takes twenty minutes for my eyes to adjust, for the real night to reveal itself. And there it is: the same sky that watched over Caesar and Cleopatra, that guided explorers and inspired poets. It’s still there, patient, waiting behind the glow. We just have to be willing to step out of the light, to stand quietly, and to remember how to look. In reclaiming the dark, I think we reclaim a piece of our own wonder.

We’ve traded that profound darkness for convenience, for safety, for the small comfort of a streetlamp’s orange pool. And I understand why. But I wonder what we lose when we banish the night. Without the deep dark, we never truly look up. We lose the shock, the awe, the humbling scale of the Milky Way sprayed across the heavens. We forget our smallness in a good way. Our problems shrink under the gaze of a billion ancient stars.

Sometimes I drive out past the last service station, where the road narrows and the artificial light finally fades. I turn off the engine and step out. It takes twenty minutes for my eyes to adjust, for the real night to reveal itself. And there it is: the same sky that watched over Caesar and Cleopatra, that guided explorers and inspired poets. It’s still there, patient, waiting behind the glow. We just have to be willing to step out of the light, to stand quietly, and to remember how to look. In reclaiming the dark, I think we reclaim a piece of our own wonder.
